chunk:
CHUNK:008/021
And then I relaxed, as I convinced myself I wouldn\u2019t need a sandwich or a snooze after. And, honestly, who was I kidding? Did I really think I was going to sleep once I got my hands on Constable Paniula\u2019s diary? Nope. It was highly improbable. Just as improbable as the idea of a Robin Hood pickpocket filling the pockets of well-heeled tourists with surprisingly expensive jewellery in Greenland. \u201cAre those diamonds?\u201d I asked when Mary showed me the ring she had found in her pocket. \u201cFake,\u201d Wilbur said. \u201cI\u2019m sure of it.\u201d I wasn\u2019t. And if it wasn\u2019t for Paniula\u2019s diary, I just knew I would have been even more intrigued by diamond-studded jewellery turning up in the pockets of Dreamcaster\u2019s passengers. But the wind was increasing in strength and the waves were growing bigger by the minute. I made a few more notes, took a few more names \u2013 also from Wisconsin \u2013 and then made a rather abrupt but hopefully professional exit, and headed for the path to Tasiusaq. I took a wrong turn up a sheep path, corrected, and then started to run. Time was running out, and the Americans were coming. \n 6 I\u2019ve lost count of all the shifts I\u2019ve worked with Atii when she drives and I run, but as I ran along the path, skirting around slow-moving clumps and clusters of red and yellow tourists, I owed her one. Sure, I was sweaty, and I realised I should have left my jacket in the dinghy, but I was really moving. Running while on patrol so often had also helped me refine the weight and balance of my utility belt. If I wasn\u2019t so hot, I might even have enjoyed the streamlined feel of my belt with no unnecessary movement; I didn\u2019t even have to press my hand on the grip of my holster, which was a first. The breeze helped keep my face cool, puffing small clouds of dust from the trail as I ran. I almost had enough excess energy to take in my surroundings \u2013 the bald granite peaks and tough green grasses that bent in the wind \u2013 but then I spotted the cabin, and it was all I could think of. I pushed on, vaguely aware that I had reached the halfway point between Qassiarsuk and Tasiusaq, and the two finger fjords on either side, and then took a hard left to jog down an uneven track to the cabin. It was, like many small cabins in the wilds of Greenland, in need of some serious, tender loving care. Flaps of bitumen on the roof lifted and clapped in the wind, and the walls were typically blistered from long summers of endless sunshine, drying and peeling the paint, followed by autumn rains and the deep, bone-chilling frost of winter to finish the job. I paused at the door, noted the frail hinges, and then lifted the handle up to open the door. Lifting the handle was supposed to deter polar bears, and it usually confounded more than a few tourists, but not the two elderly passengers sitting inside the cabin. They started as I barged in, and I caught that strange but wholly familiar sense of d\u00e9j\u00e0 vu as I recalled Uiluiit\u2019s story about passengers in the cabin in need of evacuation. I\u2019ll admit, given the time I had already wasted on the beach, I didn\u2019t really want to waste any more time coordinating a rescue. Harsh, I know, but a quick look at the passengers revealed no immediate signs of distress. At least, no distress for them. My heart beat even faster than it had on the run along the path when I spotted the woman sitting on a rickety chair at the desk \u2013 the only pieces of furniture inside the cabin \u2013 as she delicately turned the mildewed pages of what could only be Constable Paniula\u2019s diary. The man had his back to me as he studied the sheets of newspaper that had been stuck on the bare wooden walls like cheap wallpaper. I didn\u2019t doubt the newspapers were old, and I was sure he was entertained, but he was also shocked at my abrupt entry, and, as they both looked up, I felt compelled to offer an explanation. \u201cHi,\u201d I said, catching my breath and hoping to set them at ease with what had to be the most forced frivolity I had ever injected into my voice. Okay, frivolity and injected weren\u2019t words I often used together, or, in the case of frivolity, ever, but I was on the clock and the woman had the diary I needed. \u201cHello,\u201d the man said, while the woman simply stared at me. \u201cI\u2019m with the police,\u201d I said, sticking to English. \u201cSo we can see,\u201d the man said. \u201cAnd, well\u2026\u201d I took a breath and pointed at the diary. \u201cI need that,\u201d I said. \u201cIt\u2019s, ah\u2026\u201d \u201cEvidence?\u201d the man suggested. \u201cYes,\u201d I said, taking another breath. \u201cJoyce,\u201d the man said. \u201cAre you finished with the book?\u201d \u201cYes,\u201d Joyce said. She closed the diary and nodded. \u201cI think so. I can\u2019t read whatever language it is in, anyway.\u201d She handed me the diary and said, \u201cIs it important?\u201d Paniula\u2019s diary was heavy in my hands with a rough surface that seemed to prick my skin, letting the words seep through my fingers and into my body. I know how that must sound, but that was what it felt like, and the sensation was so strong, I didn\u2019t hear what the woman said, and merely grunted in response. \u201cI would take that as a yes, my love,\u201d the man said, and I looked up. He caught my eye, smiled, and then made a shooing gesture at the door. \u201cIt\u2019s all right, Officer. You look like you\u2019re very busy. Joyce and I were just taking a breather before pushing on for the other village. The one that starts with a \u2018T\u2019.\u201d \u201cTasiusaq,\u201d I said, remembering my manners.
